paragraphs.text: NLdigital
Regarding: Digital Fairness Act Consultation
Town: Breukelen, The Netherlands
Date: 23 oktober 2025

As NLdigital, the leading trade association of the digital industry in the Netherlands, we attach great value to strong consumer protection and a well-functioning digital internal market. For this reason, we wish to share our concerns about the necessity and proportionality of the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA).

Europe already has one of the most comprehensive consumer protection systems in the world. Instruments such as the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the Price Indication Directive (PID), the Digital Services Act (DSA), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the ePrivacy Directive, the Digital Markets Act (DMA), and the AI- and Data Act provide robust protection.

section_path: Our message: existing frameworks are sufficient

paragraphs.text: The challenges identified by the Commission – such as dark patterns, addictive design, harmful personalization, and misleading pricing practices – are challenges we recognize, and which must be addressed where necessary. However, these challenges are already covered sufficiently by existing, solid legislative frameworks. The problem lies in inconsistent and fragmented enforcement. New rules would be redundant and lead to more legal uncertainty. This would harm the competitive position and innovative capacity of Europe.

section_path: Our message: existing frameworks are sufficient

paragraphs.text: We call on the European Commission to draw attention to the following three points in European decision-making:

evidence_quotes.quote: more coordinated and harmonized application of existing rules.

paragraphs.text: Strengthen enforcement: invest in more coordinated and harmonized application of existing rules.
Improve cooperation: prevent fragmentation through closer cooperation between authorities and Member States.
Stimulate simplification: ensure digital information provision by default and phase out outdated obligations.

If the Digital Fairness Act is presented as a legislative proposal, it needs to be targeted and proportionate, otherwise it threatens to be counterproductive and lead to unintended consequences. By choosing better enforcement of existing rules and simplification of the legal framework, we strengthen consumer protection, preserve the innovative capacity of our businesses, and contribute to a strong and competitive Europe.
In this letter, below, you will find a brief analysis of every topic.
Kind regards,
NLdigital
NLdigital
De Corridor 5
3621 ZA Breukelen
info@nldigital.nl
Existing rules already prohibit this.
DMA art. 13: obligations to ensure fair interface design (related to interoperability and data use).

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative techniques likely to cause harm to users

paragraphs.text: AI Act art. 5(1)(a) and (b): bans AI systems that use subliminal or manipulative techniques likely to cause harm to users.
Data act recital 38: addresses fairness in design and access to data.
UCPD art. 6–9 prohibits misleading practices, intimidation, and undue influence.
Annex I: list of prohibited misleading practices
DSA art. 25 prohibits interface designs that manipulate choices.
GDPR requires free, informed, and unambiguous consent, which excludes misleading consent mechanisms.

evidence_quotes.quote: Art. 5(1): data protection principles - transparency and fairness.

paragraphs.text: Art. 5(1): data protection principles - transparency and fairness.

evidence_quotes.quote: data protection by design and by default.

paragraphs.text: Art. 25: data protection by design and by default.

evidence_quotes.quote: definition and conditions for "freely given consent"

paragraphs.text: Art. 4(11) and 7: definition and conditions for "freely given consent"
CRD art. 22 prohibits pre-checked boxes and sets clear requirements for buttons and information provision.
Art 6 (information obligations (pre-contract)) + art 8 (formal requirements (clarity and visibility))

evidence_quotes.quote: New legislation on this point is unnecessary and may undermine legitimate design choices that improve user experience.

paragraphs.text: New legislation on this point is unnecessary and may undermine legitimate design choices that improve user experience.

evidence_quotes.quote: Unfair pricing practices are already prohibited under the CRD, UCPD, and the Price Indication Directive (PID).
Dynamic pricing comes in a wide range of forms applied varyingly in different industries or business models.

paragraphs.text: Unfair pricing practices are already prohibited under the CRD, UCPD, and the Price Indication Directive (PID). Dynamic pricing comes in a wide range of forms applied varyingly in different industries or business models. Therefore, a broad restriction of the practice should be avoided as dynamic pricing can have significant benefits for consumers and businesses.

For example, it can lead to better and faster allocation of resources as it improves the matching of fluctuating demand and supply, lower prices for consumers, intensified competition between businesses, easier entry by new businesses by reducing the cost of setting and changing prices, and improved inventory management. What is needed is better enforcement – not new rules.

evidence_quotes.quote: such as recommendations or dynamic content.

paragraphs.text: A distinction must be made between risky practices and legitimate features that increase ease of use and engagement, such as recommendations or dynamic content. The DSA already provides specific protection for minors:

evidence_quotes.quote: design of an interface that "deceives or manipulates" users or "materially distorts" decisions.

paragraphs.text: Art. 25: prohibits design of an interface that "deceives or manipulates" users or "materially distorts" decisions.
Art. 28: already ensures that online platforms take appropriate measures to protect minors.
Broad prohibitions would have disproportionate consequences for industries where "addictive use" is not an issue.

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalization offers significant benefits to consumers and businesses: it prevents fraud, increases relevance, limits irrelevant advertising, and supports loyalty.

paragraphs.text: Personalization offers significant benefits to consumers and businesses: it prevents fraud, increases relevance, limits irrelevant advertising, and supports loyalty. There is already a strong framework governing personalisation.
GDPR art. 5(1) and 22 regulate profiling and personalized pricing.
GDPR prohibits the use of sensitive personal data and restricts profiling of children.

evidence_quotes.quote: profiling of minors based on sensitive data

paragraphs.text: DSA prohibits profiling of minors based on sensitive data and adds transparency requirements.
Art. 26: follows transparency obligations for advertising.

evidence_quotes.quote: opt out of recommendations based on profiling.

paragraphs.text: Art 38: follows the right for users to opt out of recommendations based on profiling.
DMA art. 15 requires gatekeepers to make their profiling mechanisms transparent.

evidence_quotes.quote: processing personal data for online advertising services without consent.

paragraphs.text: Art 5(2)(a): bans processing personal data for online advertising services without consent.
Additional rules under the DFA would lead to overlap, ambiguity, and loss of legal predictability.

evidence_quotes.quote: reversing the burden of proof would lead to legal uncertainty and disproportionate burdens

paragraphs.text: Concepts such as "fairness by design," a redefinition of the "average consumer," an expanded category of "vulnerable consumers" (for example, emotional stress) or reversing the burden of proof would lead to legal uncertainty and disproportionate burdens, especially for SME businesses:

evidence_quotes.quote: data protection by design and by default

paragraphs.text: GDPR art. 25: "data protection by design and by default" already embodies a fairness principle.
Vulnerable consumers are overall already covered in:
GDPR (e.g. special protection for children, data minimization principle).

evidence_quotes.quote: protections for minors and sensitive data

paragraphs.text: DSA (protections for minors and sensitive data).
Accessibility Act (design obligations for at-risk users).

evidence_quotes.quote: collect more data to identify "vulnerable" consumers

paragraphs.text: There is a risk of contradiction with GDPR's data minimization principle if businesses are required to collect more data to identify "vulnerable" consumers
These proposals are contrary to the Draghi report, which specifically calls for simplification and reduction of regulatory burden.

The real challenge is fragmented and inconsistent enforcement. The same practice, such as personalization, may be assessed differently by privacy supervisors, consumer authorities, and DSA coordinators. This reduces predictability and hampers innovation.

Strengthening the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Regulation would bring improvement here: better cooperation between Member States, application of the one-stop-shop principle, and consistent enforcement throughout Europe.
Kind regards,
Dagmar Lens, Director NLdigital
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
